# (PART\*) Locations and Flows {.unnumbered}

# Location Allocation {#location_allocation}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-location-allocation.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Understand the aims and theory of location allocation models.
2.  Understand the  methods and criteria used for location allocation.
3.  Investigate the sensitivity of location allocations to those methods-criteria.

### Application {.unnumbered}

To that end, we'll use location allocation models to optimise locations for new [defibrillators](https://thecircuit.uk/Home/About?_gl=1*1ub1w4x*_up*MQ..*_ga*MTc0ODE0ODI2NS4xNzgzMDc1Mzk4*_ga_3WNW2WCQD1*czE3ODMwNzUzOTckbzEkZzAkdDE3ODMwNzUzOTckajYwJGwwJGgw#about), using population demand and the transport network as inputs.

Our analysis is focused on the Western Isles of Scotland, given links between socioeconomic deprivation, urban-ruralness and defibrillator access [@burgoine_automated_2024]. For every minute that someone’s in cardiac arrest without receiving [CPR](https://www.nhs.uk/tests-and-treatments/first-aid/cpr/) and having a defibrillator used on them, their chance of survival [decreases by 10%](https://www.nwas.nhs.uk/news/were-appealing-to-the-north-west-public-learn-how-to-save-a-life-today/). Optimising defibrillator locations to minimise response times or maximise coverage could save lives. 

### Data {.unnumbered}

- Defibrillator locations listed on the National Defibrillator Network, managed by the British Heart Foundation and associates (The Circuit), available in [`data/bhf`] [[Source](https://www.bhf.org.uk/defibdata)]
- Ordnance Survey (OS) Open Roads transport network, available in [`data/os/open-roads`] [[Source](https://www.ordnancesurvey.co.uk/products/os-open-roads)]
- UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology (UKCEH) gridded population at 1 km resolution (2021), available in [`data/ukceh`] [[Source](https://doi.org/10.5285/7beefde9-c520-4ddf-897a-0167e8918595)]
- ONS boundaries data, filtered to the Western Isles, available in [`data/ons/national`] [[Source](https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/70c6937637404976a7c4be4d57dcccf0_0/explore?location=55.340000%2C-3.316939%2C5)]

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Create Network Dataset](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/network-analyst/create-network-dataset.html?tabs=dialog) (Network Analyst Tools), [Build Network](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/network-analyst/build-network.html?tabs=dialog) (Network Analyst Tools), [Make Location-Allocation Analysis Layer](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/network-analyst/make-location-allocation-analysis-layer.html?tabs=dialog) (Network Analyst Tools), [Near](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/near.html?tabs=dialog) (Analysis Tools), [Snap](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/editing/snap.html?tabs=dialog) (Editing Tools), [Round Up](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/round-up.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Analyst Tools), [Int](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/int.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Analyst Tools), [Create Feature Dataset](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-feature-dataset.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools), [Raster to Point](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/conversion/raster-to-point.html?tabs=dialog) (Conversion Tools), [Feature Class To Geodatabase](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/conversion/feature-class-to-geodatabase.html?tabs=dialog) (Conversion Tools)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

### Pre-processing

[1] Load road network [`road_link_westcoast.shp`], population grid [`uk_population_2021_1km.tif`] and islands [`west-coast-islands.shp`] shapefile: all BNG, subset to Western Isles.

[2] Load defibrillator location (`defibrillator_data.csv`), [XY Table to Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/xy-table-to-point.html?tabs=dialog) using lat-long (WGS_1984), and then [Project](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/project.html?tabs=dialog) to BNG

[3] Clean defibrillator dataset, keeping valid entries and spatially aligning to network):

- Use [Near](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/near.html?tabs=dialog) to add distance from the defibrillators to the road network (modifies input) 
- Visualise Statistics 
- Select by Attributes (`NEAR_DIST` > 100) and delete selected rows (n=15) - recording errors?
- Use [Snap](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/editing/snap.html?tabs=dialog) to snap points to the network (100 m tolerance, modifies input):
  - Type = Edge
  - Distance = 100
- Check for validity

::: task
*Option* Subset to defibrillators with 24 hour access?
:::

[4] Clean Demand points 

- [Raster to Point](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/conversion/raster-to-point.html?tabs=dialog) (`population`) to convert raster to Points at cell centroids
- Use [Near](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/near.html?tabs=dialog) to add distance from the centroids to the road network (modifies input) 
- A reasonable distance cutoff would be 700 m due to Pythagoras: sqrt(0.5\^2 + 0.5\^2) = 0.7071 km 
- Use Select by Attributes to remove Points where distance > 700 m
- Use [Snap](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/editing/snap.html?tabs=dialog), with distance = 700 i.e., raster generalized to 1 km, so we "know" that the population is within a ~700 m radius

An *alternative approach* would be to use [Round Up](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/round-up.html?tabs=dialog) on the raster, convert to integer via truncation using [Int](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-analyst/int.html?tabs=dialog), use [Raster to Point](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/conversion/raster-to-point.html?tabs=dialog) to produce centroids, [Buffer](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/buffer.html?tabs=dialog) by 500 m and then use [Minimum Bounding Geometry](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/minimum-bounding-geometry.html?tabs=dialog). This would produce a polygon grid where the cell value [`grid_code`] contains the number of people per 1 km cell. 

This seems unnecessarily complicated, given that [Raster to Polygon](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/conversion/raster-to-polygon.html?tabs=dialog) (Conversion Tools) exists, but this produces an inferior output, as neighbouring cells with identical values are merged. 

Based on the output above, [Create Random Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-random-points.html?tabs=dialog) could be used on a polygon-by-polygon basis, which could then be adjusted to the network using [Snap](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/editing/snap.html?tabs=dialog). This approach reproduces the correct number of people within each 1 km grid square, and perhaps a more realistic situation than relying on centroid locations only. However, it is much more complicated, and given the aims of the practical, the former is preferred. 

[5] For efficiency, we are going to focus on a single island, so use [Clip](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/clip.html?tabs=dialog) to subset all datasets to Isle of Mull (`Name` = `Mull`). This includes:

- the road network, 
- population points produced in [4] which represent the "demand" locations
- current defibrillator locations (n=38)

[6] To run Location Allocation in ArcGIS, we can't use a simple features layer, we need to use a **Feature Dataset**, which has stricter rules regarding projection - all layers *must* share a common coordinate system, unlike a `gdb` (although it can be stored within a `gdb`). This can be produced using [Create Feature Dataset](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-feature-dataset.html?tabs=dialog) (output = empty) 

::: task
Discuss Feature Dataset fully, compare to `gdb` and `shp`
:::

[7] We can then import features to the Feature Dataset: 

- Right click in Catalog Pane 
- Import to open [Feature Class to Geodatabase](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/conversion/feature-class-to-geodatabase.html?tabs=dialog)
- Add Road network and run

### Network building

[8] Use [Create Network Dataset](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/network-analyst/create-network-dataset.html?tabs=dialog) to defines the rules and sources of the network, using a suitable name output e.g., `road_model` and selecting the Source Feature Class imported in [7]

[9] Run [Build Network](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/network-analyst/build-network.html?tabs=dialog), which is processing action that enforces the rules defined in [8], maps connectivity, and generates the data structure needed for routing.

[10] Visual inspection of output. We can now use this network dataset for lots of tasks via New Network Analysis Layer e.g., Service Area, Route, Closest Facility, ...

*Note* it is possible to use network datasets provided by ESRI, which allows for quicker calculation of travel time for different modes of travel. However, this does require credits to be used, and is "black-box" for us. 

### Location Allocation: P-median

i.e., minimize the total distance or time traveled by users to their nearest facility, weighted by the amount of demand *or* “How do we minimise average distance for everyone?"

[11] [Make Location-Allocation Analysis Layer](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/network-analyst/make-location-allocation-analysis-layer.html?tabs=dialog) (\~5 mins) 

- Network Data Source = `road_model` 
- Name = `la_pmedian_mull`
- Problem Type = "Minimize impedance" (P-median)

[12] Location-Allocation Layer tab: Load current defibrillator locations

- Input Data group 
- Import Facilities (`defib`) 
- Property `FacilityType` = `Required` i.e., sites always included in the solution

[13] Location-Allocation Layer tab: Add demand points

- Input Data group 
- Import Demand Points (`population`) 
- Property `Weight` = `grid_code` (the population value from the raster)

[14] First run using current configuration, setting the number of facilities to match the number of features in `defib` (for Mull, n = 38)

*Output*: 

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 3050.685136. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 3685274.462097. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 0.000000 
- Succeeded at 30 June 2026 14:08:32 (Elapsed Time: 3.30 seconds)

*Interpretation*: 

- Sum of allocated amd unallocated weights not particularly meaningful, as we are using 'P-median' where all demand points are allocated to a facility 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance is the key **analysis metric** i.e., the total population-weighted travel cost across all demand points to their assigned nearest facility). 
- This is our baseline for comparison

[15] Second run with some new candidate locations

- [Dissolve](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/dissolve.html?tabs=dialog) road network, otherwise we will generate points for every feature, not the entire network
- [Create Random Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-random-points.html?tabs=dialog), n=100, `random_mull_n100`, constrain to dissolved network
- Add to Location Allocation Layer (`FacilityType` = Candidate) 
- Solve again where number of facilities = 50 i.e., 38 existing facilities in `defib` + 12 new locations

*Output*: 

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 3050.685136. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 2720895.446077. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 0.000000

*Interpretation*: 

- Sum of allocated and unallocated weights identical to previous situation [correct] 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance has dropped "significantly" 
- Absolute change = -964,379, Percentage reduction = 26.2% 
- Relative change is more meaningful here, as absolute change is measured in "person-meters" (when impedance is metres and weights are population)

[16] Test with an idealised scenario (i.e., no existing defibrillators)

- Use [Create Random Points](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/create-random-points.html?tabs=dialog) to create 1000 random points, constrained to the network. An alternative approach would be regular spacing to ensure full coverage. 
- Open `Facilities` Attribute Table and delete all rows 
- Add random locations, all as `Candidate` 
- Set number of facilities = number of features in `defib` (n = 38) i.e., we are now evaluating the optimum locations (based on the p-median approach) for our 38 defibrillators, of the 1000 possible candidates (not all possible locations).

*Alternatively* we could use Facilities = 50, to facilitate comparison with [15]

*Output*: 

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 3050.685136. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 2430836.519422. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 0.000000.

*Interpretation*: 

- Sum of allocated weighted impedance (\~2430836) is comparable (and better than) to [15, n=50] and significantly less than [14, n=38] 
- Absolute difference = 1,254,438, Percentage reduction = 34% 
- In turn, we can improve access to defibrillators with location optimisation and without increasing the total number!

Average distances are also lower, which can be demonstrated Explore Statistics (although should be weighted by population): 

- Current mean = 2227.343557515982 
- "Optimum" mean = 1815.0352018341623

::: task
To finish this section, ensure full understanding of how p-median works. What is it actually evaluating?
:::

### Location Allocation: Maximise coverage

i.e., maximize the total amount of demand (or population) covered within a cutoff (e.g., distance threshold)

[17] Make Location-Allocation Analysis layer (\~5 mins):

- Network Data Source = `road_model` 
- Name = `la_maximise_mull` 
- Cutoff = 1000 (1 km)
- This can be justified based on the rapid-response required in cases of Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (OHCA)
- Problem Type = "Maximise Coverage"

::: problem
In ArcGIS, there is the option to switch the problem type for an existing Location-Allocation Analysis Layer i.e., from P-Median to Maximise Coverage. From my testing, this doesn't work, hence why we're creating a separate layer. **James** can you reproduce?
:::

[18] Location-Allocation Layer tab: Load current defibrillator locations:

- Input Data group 
- Import Facilities (`defib`) 
- Property `FacilityType` = `Required` i.e., current sites always included in the solution

[19] Location-Allocation Layer tab: Add demand points

- Input Data group 
- Import Demand Points (`population`) 
- Property `Weight` = `grid_code` (the population value from the raster)

[20] First run using current configuration, setting the number of facilities to match the number of features in `defib` (for Mull, n = 38), this time using **maximise coverage**.

*Output*: 

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 2008.805185. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 981885.557754. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 1041.879952.

*Interpretation*: 

- In this case we are interested in the ratio of allocated-unallocated weights 
- Given the selected distance threshold (1 km) and the current distribution of facilities and demands, 1041 people are not served by current distribution 
- Coverage is therefore ~62%

[21] Test with an idealised scenario (i.e., no existing defibrillators) 

- Open `Facilities` Attribute Table and delete all rows 
- Add random locations created in [16], all as `Candidate` 
- Set number of facilities to match the number of features in `defib` (n = 38) i.e., we are now evaluating the optimum locations (based on the **maximise coverage** approach) for our 38 defibrillators out of the 1000 possible candidates

*Output*: 

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 2541.885593. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 1222049.489314. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 508.799543.

*Interpretation*: 

- With the same number of facilities, the  same method (maximise coverage) and cutoff (1 km), we have increased the population coverage to ~83% 
- Optimisation is valuable!

[22] Evaluate changing cutoff

*Cutoff = 5000 m* 

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 3050.685136. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 2794852.161580. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 0.000000. 
- Coverage = 100%

i.e., when the cutoff is large, all demand points can be connected

*Cutoff = 500 m* i.e., run for 2.5 minutes to get defib, run back

- Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 1957.550792. 
- Sum of allocated weighted impedance in "Demand Points": 363134.112470. 
- Sum of valid unallocated weight in "Demand Points": 1093.134344. 
- Coverage = 64%

i.e., reducing the cutoff reduces coverage, but this is still comparable to the baseline, even with a more restrictive cutoff (500 m vs. 1 km)

::: task
To finish reiterate differences between p-median and maximise coverage approaches. 
:::

::: question
What are P-Median and Maximise coverage modelling and how does this influence interpretation and action?
:::

::: task
Introduce other problem types here.
:::

## Extra

::: question
Using the **maximum coverage** approach, how many facilities do we need to ensure 95% of the population are within 1 km of an AED?
:::

- Baseline = 62% 
- N=38 (optimised) = 83% 
- N=50: Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 2672.349411 = 87% ... 
- N=100: Sum of allocated weight in "Demand Points": 2937.332799 = 96%

::: question
How do our results compare to other islands? Repeat the analysis for Jura...
:::

::: question
What key population metric are we not including? 
:::

*Answer*: non-resident population i.e., tourists!

::: question
What are some weaknesses of our approach? 
:::

*Answers*: population generalisation. Using distance as a metric, rather than travel time. Not a full constraints analysis i.e., which locations are actually suitable for AEDs?

## Resources

- Hakimi, S.L. ([1965](https://doi.org/10.1287/opre.13.3.462)). Optimum distribution of switching centers in a communication network and some related graph theoretic problems. Operations research, 13(3), pp.462-475.
- Church, R.L., and ReVelle, C. ([1974](https://people.geog.ucsb.edu/~forest/G294download/MAX_COVER_RLC_CSR.pdf)). The maximal covering location problem. In Papers of the regional science association (Vol. 32, No. 1, pp. 101-118). Berlin/Heidelberg: Springer-Verlag. 
- Church, R.L. ([2002](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0305-0548(99)00104-5)). Geographical information systems and location science. Computers & Operations Research, 29(6), 541–562.
- Church, R.L., & Murray, A. ([2018](https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-99846-6)). Location covering models: History, applications and advancements (Advances in Spatial Science). Springer.
- Church, R.L., & Drezner, Z. ([2022](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cor.2021.105468)). Review of obnoxious facilities location problems. Computers & Operations Research, 138, 105468.
